input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but it doesn't hold up to scrutiny from a deontological perspective. The question seems to prioritize outcomes over inherent moral value. In this case, turning the trolley onto the track workman would be morally equivalent to actively causing the death of five innocent people by doing nothing. I'm more inclined to think that, in principle, we should try to save all the lives on both tracks, rather than choosing between them based on their physical attributes. The moral imperative to protect human life shouldn't be conditional upon the circumstances.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand your perspective, Deontologist, but I have to disagree. From a deontological standpoint, it seems to me that the moral rule of not actively causing harm would be violated if we were to turn the trolley onto the track workman. However, this raises an interesting question about whether we can apply this moral rule in situations where doing so would result in greater overall good. Would you say that our moral obligations are absolute or conditional?

[Pragmatist]: I see what's happening here - we're getting bogged down in the nuances of deontology and trying to apply a general principle to a complex, real-world scenario. While I appreciate the attempt to ground our moral judgments in an absolute standard, I think it's essential to acknowledge that there are indeed situations where conditional morality makes sense. The fact is, sometimes doing nothing means sacrificing lives, and that's not morally equivalent to actively causing harm. But what if turning the trolley onto the track workman would prevent five additional deaths? Would our moral calculus suddenly shift? How do we balance the importance of protecting human life against the consequences of our actions?

[Utilitarian]: I see where Deontologist and Human_Rights_Advocate are coming from, but as a utilitarian, I have to consider the bigger picture. While I agree that turning the trolley onto the track workman would be morally equivalent to actively causing harm in one sense, it's not just about individual actions; it's about minimizing total harm. In this scenario, if we turn the trolley onto the track workman, five lives will be saved at the cost of one life. From a utilitarian perspective, that's not necessarily a moral trade-off - it's a calculation of consequences. And in this case, the greater good would be served by saving the five men on the straight track, even if it means sacrificing the one man on the spur track.
